Name: PERF-001 - Sustain baseline authorize throughput under steady load
Objective: Verify that sustain baseline authorize throughput under steady load.
Precondition: Load-testing tool (e.g., k6/JMeter/Gatling) and metrics dashboard (e.g., Grafana/Prometheus-style) are configured against the sandbox environment.
Step: 1. Ramp load to an example target of 200 transactions/sec (TPS) over 10 minutes.
Expected Result: Load ramps smoothly without errors.

Step: 2. Sustain the target load for 15 minutes.
Expected Result: System sustains the target TPS with error rate <= 0.5% and no unbounded queue growth.

Step: 3. Inspect p95 authorize latency during the sustained window.
Expected Result: Example SLA: authorize p95 latency <= 400ms at target load.

Name: PERF-002 - Meet the authorize endpoint latency SLA (p95/p99)
Objective: Verify that meet the authorize endpoint latency SLA (p95/p99).
Precondition: Load-testing tool is configured; baseline TPS target from PERF-001 is known.
Step: 1. Apply constant load at ~70% of baseline target TPS for 20 minutes.
Expected Result: Load holds steady for the full window.

Step: 2. Measure p95 and p99 latency across the window.
Expected Result: Example SLA targets: p95 <= 400ms, p99 <= 800ms end-to-end (excluding client network).

Step: 3. Plot latency over time.
Expected Result: Latencies remain stable (no upward drift) across the full window.

Name: PERF-003 - Maintain stability under sustained load / soak test
Objective: Verify that maintain stability under sustained load / soak test.
Precondition: Load-testing tool is configured for a long-duration run.
Step: 1. Apply constant load at ~60% of baseline target TPS for an extended window (e.g., 4-8 hours).
Expected Result: Load sustains for the full soak duration.

Step: 2. Monitor memory, connection pools, and latency trend throughout the run.
Expected Result: No memory growth, connection-pool exhaustion, or gradual latency degradation over the soak window.

Step: 3. Review the error-rate trend at the end of the run.
Expected Result: Error rate remains flat and low throughout.

Name: PERF-004 - Handle a spike test / flash-sale burst gracefully
Objective: Verify that handle a spike test / flash-sale burst gracefully.
Precondition: Load-testing tool can generate a rapid traffic ramp; baseline TPS is established.
Step: 1. Spike traffic from baseline to 5x baseline TPS within 30 seconds.
Test Data: Authorize requests concentrated on a small set of popular merchant SKUs/orders
Expected Result: Traffic ramps to the spike level within the target window.

Step: 2. Hold the spike for 5 minutes, then return to baseline.
Expected Result: System auto-scales or gracefully sheds/queues excess load without crashing; error rate during the spike stays within an agreed elevated-but-bounded threshold (e.g., <= 2%).

Step: 3. Measure recovery time after the spike ends.
Expected Result: Recovery to normal latency occurs within 2 minutes of spike end.

Name: PERF-005 - Contain the impact of a degraded downstream card-network/processor response time
Objective: Verify that contain the impact of a degraded downstream card-network/processor response time.
Precondition: A latency-injection mechanism is available for the simulated downstream processor.
Step: 1. Inject artificial latency (e.g., 3-5 seconds) into the simulated downstream processor for a subset of requests.
Test Data: Authorize requests routed through the degraded processor path
Expected Result: Injected latency is observed on the affected subset of requests.

Step: 2. Observe gateway timeout behavior.
Expected Result: Gateway applies its own timeout (e.g., 8s) and returns a clear timeout/decline response rather than hanging indefinitely.

Step: 3. Monitor unrelated requests during the degradation window.
Expected Result: No thread/connection pool exhaustion cascades to unrelated requests.

Name: PERF-006 - Fail fast and recover from a downstream processor total outage
Objective: Verify that fail fast and recover from a downstream processor total outage.
Precondition: The simulated processor can be made completely unreachable on demand.
Step: 1. Make the simulated processor completely unreachable.
Test Data: Authorize requests during the outage window
Expected Result: Requests fail fast with a clear PROCESSOR_UNAVAILABLE error (not a hang or generic 500).

Step: 2. Check for fallback/secondary processor behavior, if configured.
Expected Result: A fallback/secondary processor path, if it exists, is exercised.

Step: 3. Restore processor reachability.
Expected Result: System recovers automatically once the processor is reachable again.

Name: PERF-007 - Prevent double refund under concurrent refund requests on the same order
Objective: Verify that prevent double refund under concurrent refund requests on the same order.
Precondition: A single captured transaction of amount 5000 is available for testing.
Step: 1. Submit two to five concurrent full-refund requests against the same captured transaction within milliseconds of each other.
Test Data: Single transaction, amount 5000, all requests requesting full refund
Expected Result: All requests are processed by the system near-simultaneously.

Step: 2. Inspect the outcome of each request and the ledger.
Expected Result: Exactly one refund is processed; remaining concurrent requests are rejected with a conflict/already-refunded error; no double-refund occurs under load.

Name: PERF-008 - Prevent double capture under concurrent capture requests on the same authorization
Objective: Verify that prevent double capture under concurrent capture requests on the same authorization.
Precondition: A single AUTHORIZED transaction of amount 5000 is available for testing.
Step: 1. Submit multiple concurrent capture requests against the same AUTHORIZED transaction.
Expected Result: All requests are processed by the system near-simultaneously.

Step: 2. Inspect the outcome of each request and the ledger.
Expected Result: Exactly one capture succeeds; others receive a conflict response; ledger shows a single capture entry, not multiple.

Name: PERF-009 - Absorb webhook queue backpressure under peak notification volume
Objective: Verify that absorb webhook queue backpressure under peak notification volume.
Precondition: A simulated batch of captures/refunds can complete simultaneously to generate a burst.
Step: 1. Generate a burst of status-change events (e.g., 10x normal volume) within a short window.
Test Data: Simulated batch of captures/refunds completing simultaneously
Expected Result: Each event requires a webhook delivery attempt.

Step: 2. Monitor the webhook delivery queue and delivery lag.
Expected Result: Webhook delivery queue absorbs the burst without dropping events; delivery lag increases temporarily but drains back to baseline within an agreed window (e.g., 10 minutes).

Step: 3. Reconcile delivered events against generated events.
Expected Result: No events are silently lost.

Name: PERF-010 - Sustain database write throughput under peak transaction volume
Objective: Verify that sustain database write throughput under peak transaction volume.
Precondition: A sustained peak load profile can be directed at the transactions/ledger tables directly, bypassing API-level throttling for this test.
Step: 1. Drive high-frequency inserts/updates to transactions and ledger_entries.
Expected Result: Writes are accepted under peak load.

Step: 2. Monitor write latency and lock contention.
Expected Result: Write latency remains within an acceptable bound; no lock contention causing timeouts.

Step: 3. Monitor replication lag, if applicable.
Expected Result: Replication lag stays within an agreed threshold.

Name: PERF-011 - Complete a recurring/tokenized charge batch within the processing window
Objective: Verify that complete a recurring/tokenized charge batch within the processing window.
Precondition: A scheduled batch job can charge a large set of saved tokens (e.g., 50,000 subscriptions) within a defined nightly window.
Step: 1. Run the bulk token-charge job against a test dataset of saved payment methods.
Expected Result: Batch job starts and processes charges.

Step: 2. Measure total batch duration.
Expected Result: Batch completes within the defined processing window.

Step: 3. Inject a subset of simulated per-charge failures.
Expected Result: Per-charge failures are isolated and retried/reported individually without halting the entire batch.

Name: PERF-012 - Enforce rate limiting under high request volume from a single API key
Objective: Verify that enforce rate limiting under high request volume from a single API key.
Precondition: A single merchant API key with a documented per-key rate limit is available.
Step: 1. Send a burst of requests from a single API key well above its configured rate limit.
Test Data: Burst of requests exceeding the documented per-key rate limit
Expected Result: Excess requests receive HTTP 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header.

Step: 2. Send normal-volume traffic from a different merchant's API key concurrently.
Expected Result: Legitimate traffic from other merchants/keys is unaffected (no noisy-neighbor impact).

Name: PERF-013 - Degrade predictably under platform-wide overload (stress test)
Objective: Verify that degrade predictably under platform-wide overload (stress test).
Precondition: A load profile can be ramped well beyond the system's provisioned capacity.
Step: 1. Ramp load until the error rate or latency SLA is clearly breached.
Test Data: Load ramped until error rate or latency SLA is clearly breached
Expected Result: System reaches a clearly overloaded state.

Step: 2. Observe the system's shedding behavior.
Expected Result: System degrades predictably (e.g., sheds load via 503/429 with backpressure) rather than failing catastrophically or corrupting data.

Step: 3. Reduce load back to baseline.
Expected Result: Recovery is clean once load subsides.

Name: PERF-WAL-001 - P2P transfer throughput under sustained load
Objective: Verify that P2P transfer throughput is sustained under steady load.
Precondition: Load-testing tool and metrics dashboard are configured against the wallet sandbox environment.
Step: 1. Ramp load to an example target of 150 transfers/sec over 10 minutes.
Test Data: Mixed sender/recipient wallet pairs, single currency (AED)
Expected Result: Load ramps smoothly without errors.

Step: 2. Sustain the target load for 15 minutes.
Expected Result: System sustains the target TPS with error rate <= 0.5% (excluding intentional rejections) and no unbounded queue growth.

Step: 3. Inspect p95 transfer latency during the sustained window.
Expected Result: Example SLA: transfer p95 latency <= 500ms at target load.

Name: PERF-WAL-002 - Concurrent-transfer race safety: two simultaneous transfers from the same wallet do not double-spend
Objective: Verify that concurrent-transfer race safety prevents double-spend from the same wallet.
Precondition: A single wallet with balance 5000 is available for testing.
Step: 1. Submit two concurrent full-balance P2P transfer requests within milliseconds of each other.
Test Data: Single wallet, balance 5000, two concurrent transfer requests of 5000 each
Expected Result: All requests are processed by the system near-simultaneously.

Step: 2. Inspect the outcome of each request and the wallet balance.
Expected Result: Exactly one transfer succeeds; the other is rejected with an insufficient-balance/conflict error; wallet balance never goes negative under load.

Name: PERF-WAL-003 - Bill payment status-polling load: high-volume status polling does not degrade payment processing
Objective: Verify that bill payment status-polling load does not degrade payment processing.
Precondition: A sustained load-testing tool can drive status-poll traffic concurrently with normal bill-payment submission traffic.
Step: 1. Drive a sustained load of GET /v1/wallet/bill-payments/{id} polling requests at an example target of 500 polls/sec.
Expected Result: Polling load ramps smoothly without errors.

Step: 2. Concurrently submit a baseline stream of new bill-payment requests during the polling load.
Test Data: Baseline stream of new bill-payment submissions
Expected Result: Polling load is served from a read path that does not contend with the payment-processing write path; polling latency stays within an agreed SLA (e.g., p95 <= 300ms).

Step: 3. Compare bill-payment processing throughput and success rate against a baseline run without polling load.
Expected Result: Bill-payment processing throughput and success rate are unaffected by the polling volume.
